schema_notes.kill_signals: Per-pattern deterministic regex checks. Semantics: if ANY signal fires on the target source within `scope`, the candidate finding for this pattern is marked as safely excluded (Gate1 KILL). Each signal records `kind`, `pattern`, `scope`, `semantics`.
schema_notes.counter_questions: Global six-question reverse validation (workflow.md §6). Asked verbatim to the LLM for every High/Critical finding; any YES triggers downgrade or KILL (Gate2).
schema_notes.counter_question_hints: Per-pattern Solana-specific guidance for how to answer each of the six counter_questions. Fed into Gate2 prompt.
schema_notes.attack_scenario_template: Per-pattern SETUP/CALL/RESULT/COST/DETECT/NET_ROI skeleton (audit-sop.md §4.3.3). Fed into Gate3 prompt. CALL or RESULT empty after LLM fill ⇒ Gate3 KILL.
schema_notes.scope_values: `struct` = #[derive(Accounts)] block, `function_body` = pub fn body that owns the finding, `file` = whole source text

counter_questions.text: Is there an upstream check I missed that actually prevents this?
Is this documented intended behavior (program comment, design decision, on-chain governance)?
Does exploitation require admin/privileged access (multisig, upgrade authority, on-chain governance)?
Is the economic cost to exploit greater than the attacker's gain?
Was this flagged in a prior audit as acknowledged / risk-accepted?
Is the sensitive data or action already publicly visible / intended to be callable by anyone?

patterns.title: Missing signer authorization
patterns.why_it_matters: Authority-like AccountInfo fields without Signer or #[account(signer)] let callers spoof the authority account.
look_for: token transfer authorities used without signer checks
patterns.fix: Use Signer<'info>, #[account(signer)], or an explicit is_signer/require check before privileged actions.

kill_signals.semantics: Authority-like field is typed as Signer<'info>; Anchor asserts signature at deserialize time.
Anchor #[account(signer)] attribute enforces signer check.
Explicit is_signer guard before privileged code path.
require_keys_eq! pins the authority account to a fixed pubkey, preventing arbitrary-caller spoof.

counter_question_hints.q1_upstream_check: Look for Signer<'info>/#[account(signer)] on the same field, or require_keys_eq!/is_signer in the function body (incl. shared helpers).
counter_question_hints.q2_intended_behavior: Check program doc-comments for 'permissionless' / 'read-only' intent. Log-only functions without state mutation may be intentionally unsigned.
counter_question_hints.q3_admin_only: If the missing-signer field maps to an admin pubkey verified via require_keys_eq!, exploitation requires spoofing that fixed key — usually infeasible.
counter_question_hints.q4_economic: If the impacted instruction moves funds or updates authority, gain >> cost. If it only writes logs, treat as Info.
counter_question_hints.q5_ack_by_prior_audit: Cross-reference prior SolGuard reports in outputs/memory (if any) for acknowledged signer-spoof notes.
counter_question_hints.q6_public_data: If the instruction reads public state only (e.g., logs), the 'spoof' has no security impact.

attack_scenario_template.setup_hint: Attacker controls any fresh keypair (no funding needed beyond rent exemption).
attack_scenario_template.call_hint: Attacker invokes `<instruction>` passing their keypair as the `authority` account. No signature required because the struct uses `AccountInfo<'info>`.
attack_scenario_template.result_hint: Attacker is accepted as the authority and can trigger the privileged action (fund withdrawal, state mutation, authority rotation).
attack_scenario_template.typical_cost: ~5 000 lamports transaction fee; no flash-loan or bonding needed on Solana.
attack_scenario_template.typical_detect: Program has no runtime guard; off-chain monitors are the only line of defense.
attack_scenario_template.typical_net_roi: If the privileged action moves value ≥ 0.01 SOL, ROI > 1 — exploit is profitable.

patterns.title: Missing account owner validation
patterns.why_it_matters: Raw AccountInfo data can be deserialized from accounts owned by other programs unless owner is checked first.
patterns.fix: Use Account<'info, T> or require the account owner to equal the expected program id before reading or writing.

kill_signals.semantics: Anchor Account<'info, T> asserts both owner == declare_id and discriminator match.
Explicit #[account(owner = ...)] constraint lets Anchor assert owner automatically.
Manual owner-equality check against expected program id before trusting data.

counter_question_hints.q1_upstream_check: Search for Account<'info, T>, #[account(owner = ...)], or an explicit *account.owner == crate::ID comparison in the function body.
counter_question_hints.q2_intended_behavior: Cross-program data reads (oracles, registry lookups) may be intentional — but they should still verify the owner is the expected program.
counter_question_hints.q3_admin_only: Owner checks almost never require admin privilege; deserialization bugs exploitable by any caller.
counter_question_hints.q4_economic: If the deserialized struct governs funds / PDA derivations, exploitation is highly profitable.
counter_question_hints.q5_ack_by_prior_audit: Rare in prior audits; assume new unless explicitly annotated.
counter_question_hints.q6_public_data: Data stored in program state is on-chain-public, but trust semantics are violated when owner is forged — it is NOT a privacy issue.

attack_scenario_template.setup_hint: Attacker deploys a malicious program that writes crafted bytes matching the victim's struct layout to an account they own.
attack_scenario_template.call_hint: Attacker invokes `<instruction>` passing their attacker-owned account in the AccountInfo slot that gets manually deserialized.
attack_scenario_template.result_hint: Victim program treats attacker-owned data as authentic (forged authority keys, bogus balances, poisoned state).
attack_scenario_template.typical_cost: Deploy cost of crafted account: ~0.002 SOL rent; transaction fee negligible.
attack_scenario_template.typical_detect: No on-chain guard (that's the bug); only off-chain monitors see anomalous callers.
attack_scenario_template.typical_net_roi: ROI unbounded if trusted fields like `authority: Pubkey` are spoofable — attacker can hijack any downstream action.

patterns.title: Account data matching / type cosplay
patterns.why_it_matters: Programs must bind deserialized account data to the expected discriminator, owner, and semantic identity.
look_for: missing discriminator or key equality checks
patterns.fix: Use Anchor Account<T> or explicitly validate discriminator, owner, and expected keys before trusting fields.

kill_signals.semantics: Anchor Account<'info, T> asserts discriminator + owner.
Explicit discriminator check on the first 8 bytes before deserialization.
require_keys_eq! binds deserialized struct's identity field to an expected pubkey.

counter_question_hints.q1_upstream_check: Look for Account<T> wrapper, discriminator comparison, or identity field binding (require_keys_eq!).
counter_question_hints.q2_intended_behavior: Very rarely intentional; manual deserialization without identity check is almost always a defect.
counter_question_hints.q3_admin_only: No admin required — any caller can pass a spoofed struct.
counter_question_hints.q4_economic: High ROI whenever the struct fields steer value transfers or authority decisions.
counter_question_hints.q6_public_data: N/A — this is an integrity bug, not a disclosure bug.

attack_scenario_template.setup_hint: Attacker creates an account of type A where the same program (or a sister program) also expects type B; byte layout happens to overlap on critical fields.
attack_scenario_template.call_hint: Attacker invokes `<instruction>` that deserializes into type B; since identity isn't bound, B's fields resolve to attacker-controlled values.
attack_scenario_template.result_hint: Attacker's fake B passes validation; downstream logic trusts its authority/balance/flag fields.
attack_scenario_template.typical_cost: Rent for the decoy account: <= 0.005 SOL.
attack_scenario_template.typical_net_roi: High whenever the downstream function is state-mutating.

patterns.title: Arbitrary CPI target
patterns.why_it_matters: If a CPI program id comes from user-controlled accounts, attackers can route trusted account metas to malicious programs.
look_for: missing equality check against known program id
patterns.fix: Use Program<'info, Token> or require_keys_eq!(program.key(), spl_token::ID) before invoking.

kill_signals.semantics: Anchor Program<'info, X> enforces canonical program id (spl_token::ID, system_program::ID, ...).
require_keys_eq! binds the target program to a known canonical id before the invoke.
CPI target is a hard-coded canonical module path (not user-supplied account).

counter_question_hints.q1_upstream_check: Look for Program<'info, X> typing, require_keys_eq!(target.key(), <ID>) immediately before invoke, or hard-coded instruction path (spl_token::instruction::transfer).
counter_question_hints.q2_intended_behavior: Routers/aggregators sometimes accept user-provided target programs by design — but a whitelist must still exist.
counter_question_hints.q3_admin_only: Admin-only arbitrary-CPI is still catastrophic (admin rug risk); do not downgrade on Q3 alone.
counter_question_hints.q4_economic: Trivial to exploit with a minimal attacker program; cost is dwarfed by the accessible account deltas.
counter_question_hints.q5_ack_by_prior_audit: Very rarely accepted; treat as new.

attack_scenario_template.setup_hint: Attacker deploys a malicious program whose entrypoint accepts the same account metas as the target CPI (e.g. a fake SPL-token).
attack_scenario_template.call_hint: Attacker invokes `<instruction>` passing their fake program as the `token_program` / `target_program` account and the victim program as one of the account metas with attacker-friendly authority.
attack_scenario_template.result_hint: Victim program delegates authority to attacker's code, which drains the token vault (or performs any arbitrary action with victim's signer seeds).
attack_scenario_template.typical_cost: Deploy cost of malicious program: 0.01–0.1 SOL one-off.
attack_scenario_template.typical_detect: None at runtime; funds are already in attacker custody by the time off-chain monitors fire.
attack_scenario_template.typical_net_roi: ROI = entire exposed vault / deploy cost ⇒ typically ≫ 1.

patterns.title: Non-canonical or shared PDA derivation
patterns.why_it_matters: User-controlled bumps or insufficient seed domains can validate alternate PDAs or share authority across users.
patterns.fix: Use canonical bumps and Anchor seeds/bump constraints with domain-separated seeds.

kill_signals.semantics: Anchor seeds+bump constraint derives and asserts PDA canonical.
Bump is read from on-chain state (canonical) rather than user-supplied.
Program uses find_program_address, which always returns the canonical bump.

counter_question_hints.q1_upstream_check: Verify: (a) bump stored at init and reused via `#[account(seeds=..., bump = state.bump)]`, (b) seeds include per-user identifier.
counter_question_hints.q2_intended_behavior: Shared PDAs may be intentional for global counters/registries; check docs.
counter_question_hints.q3_admin_only: Usually no admin required; any user can collide PDAs.
counter_question_hints.q4_economic: Depends on what the PDA authorizes; high when the PDA is a vault signer.

attack_scenario_template.setup_hint: Attacker identifies a PDA where user identity isn't part of the seeds (or where the bump is caller-supplied).
attack_scenario_template.call_hint: Attacker invokes `<instruction>` with crafted seeds/bump to derive a PDA that normally belongs to the victim.
attack_scenario_template.result_hint: Attacker controls the derived PDA and steals its lamports / token balance or subverts its authority role.
attack_scenario_template.typical_cost: Transaction fees only; no deploy needed.
attack_scenario_template.typical_net_roi: Equal to PDA's balance or authority exposure.

patterns.title: Uninitialized or re-initializable account
patterns.why_it_matters: Manual initialization without an initialized flag or discriminator check can allow repeated initialization.
patterns.fix: Use Anchor init constraints or write and enforce an initialized discriminator before mutation.

kill_signals.semantics: Anchor #[account(init, ...)] payer/space handles initialization atomically with rent-exempt creation.
Explicit initialized-flag check blocks re-initialization.
Code checks the 8-byte discriminator is unset before writing, preventing reinit.

counter_question_hints.q1_upstream_check: Check for #[account(init, payer, space)] or an explicit `require!(!account.initialized)` guard.
counter_question_hints.q2_intended_behavior: Some programs reuse accounts intentionally (e.g., ephemeral state) — but then they must zero data, not repopulate it.
counter_question_hints.q3_admin_only: No admin required in typical cases.
counter_question_hints.q4_economic: Depends on whether re-init wipes accounting; high when balances or authorities get reset.

attack_scenario_template.setup_hint: Target account exists (closed, or partially initialized, or freshly airdropped rent).
attack_scenario_template.call_hint: Attacker invokes `initialize`/`create` again, overwriting the account struct with attacker-chosen fields.
attack_scenario_template.result_hint: Authority, mint, or bookkeeping fields flip to attacker values, letting them seize control or double-withdraw.
attack_scenario_template.typical_net_roi: Potentially equal to entire program TVL.

patterns.title: Duplicate mutable accounts
patterns.why_it_matters: Two writable accounts may alias the same address unless the program enforces distinctness.
patterns.fix: Add require_keys_neq! or Anchor account constraints to enforce distinct writable accounts.

kill_signals.semantics: require_keys_neq! enforces distinct account keys for the mutable pair.
Anchor constraint expression enforces account inequality.

counter_question_hints.q1_upstream_check: Look for require_keys_neq! between the two writable accounts, or an Anchor `constraint = a.key() != b.key()`.
counter_question_hints.q2_intended_behavior: Aliasing intentional only in zero-sum utilities; otherwise a bug.
counter_question_hints.q4_economic: Only exploitable when the instruction does arithmetic that double-counts balance; often low impact.

attack_scenario_template.setup_hint: Attacker is the owner of a single token account and the instruction has two writable `token_account` slots.
attack_scenario_template.call_hint: Attacker passes the same account in both slots; instruction reads old balance twice, writes new balance twice.
attack_scenario_template.result_hint: Second write clobbers intermediate deduction, effectively duplicating the balance.

patterns.title: Sysvar address spoofing
patterns.why_it_matters: Sysvar accounts passed as raw AccountInfo can be spoofed unless the address or Sysvar type is enforced.
patterns.fix: Use Sysvar<'info, T> or require the account key to equal the canonical sysvar id.

kill_signals.semantics: Anchor Sysvar<'info, T> asserts canonical sysvar id automatically.
Explicit equality check against canonical sysvar id.

counter_question_hints.q1_upstream_check: Look for Sysvar<'info, Clock/Rent/Instructions> typing or require_keys_eq!(.key(), sysvar::clock::ID) etc.
counter_question_hints.q2_intended_behavior: Sysvar data reads should always be authenticated; spoofing is never intentional.
counter_question_hints.q4_economic: Depends on how the sysvar data drives logic (e.g., time-based unlocks).
counter_question_hints.q6_public_data: N/A (sysvars are public but integrity matters).

attack_scenario_template.setup_hint: Attacker crafts an account mimicking the Clock/Rent sysvar layout with forged values (future timestamp, zero rent).
attack_scenario_template.call_hint: Attacker invokes `<instruction>` passing their fake sysvar; program reads attacker-chosen timestamp/rent values.
attack_scenario_template.result_hint: Time-locked actions unlock early, or rent-exemption checks are bypassed.
attack_scenario_template.typical_cost: Rent for the decoy account: ~0.002 SOL.
attack_scenario_template.typical_net_roi: Depends on the unlocked functionality; often medium.

patterns.title: Unsafe account closing / account revival
patterns.why_it_matters: Draining lamports without writing the closed-account discriminator can allow account revival.
patterns.fix: Use Anchor close constraints or zero data and write the closed-account discriminator after draining lamports.

kill_signals.semantics: Anchor #[account(close = destination)] handles lamport drain + data zeroing + discriminator wipe atomically.
Manual zeroing of account data and/or writing the closed-account discriminator sentinel.

counter_question_hints.q1_upstream_check: Look for #[account(close = dest)] or explicit data.fill(0) / CLOSED_ACCOUNT_DISCRIMINATOR write after lamport drain.
counter_question_hints.q2_intended_behavior: Never intentional — revival opportunity is always a bug.
counter_question_hints.q3_admin_only: Depends — some closes are admin-only; but attacker can still revive post-close.
counter_question_hints.q4_economic: High when the revived account controls funds or authority.

attack_scenario_template.setup_hint: After a legitimate close, the account's lamport balance drops below rent-exemption but the data + discriminator remain intact.
attack_scenario_template.call_hint: Attacker sends enough lamports to the closed account to restore rent-exemption within the same block, then invokes a subsequent instruction that reads the (stale) data as authentic.
attack_scenario_template.result_hint: Program re-accepts the resurrected account; its old authority/balance fields now steer attacker-controlled logic.
attack_scenario_template.typical_cost: Rent top-up (~0.002 SOL) + one transaction fee.
attack_scenario_template.typical_detect: None — program has no revival check.
attack_scenario_template.typical_net_roi: Equal to the account's latent authority or balance; often High.
